source.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: If He is begotten, He is Son: if He is Son, He is the “true Son,” because Only-Begotten.

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: For we also are called sons: He Son by nature, we sons by grace . . .

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: To say that because He is begotten, He is of another nature, is to deny that He is the “true Son.”

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: Now we have the Scripture: “That we may be in His true Son Jesus Christ; He is the true God and Eternal Life.”

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: Why “true God”? because “true Son” of God.

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: For if He has given to animals this property, that what they beget shall be none other than what they themselves are: man begets man, dog begets dog, and should God not beget God?

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: If then He is of the same substance, why callest thou Him less?

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: Is it because when a human father begets a son, though human beget human, yet greater begets less?

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: If so, then let us wait for Christ to grow as human beings grow whom human beings beget!

verses.book: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John.
verses.text: But if Christ, ever since He was begotten (and this was not in time but from eternity), is what He is, and yet is less than the Father, at that rate the human condition is the better of the two: for a human being at any rate can grow, and has the property of sooner or later attaining to the age, to the strength of the father; but He never: then how is He a “true Son”?
